text: (Spring Valley, NY: Anthroposophic Press, 1985) and the work of Wolfgang Schad for some clear evidence-based examples. 5. A thoughtful and beautiful, though incomplete, examination of this concept appears in The Meaning of Love by Vladimir Solovyov (Hudson, NY: Lindisfarne Press, 1985). 6. Acts of intentionality are, according to Rudolf Steiner, acts of freedom. His picture of intention and freedom is complex and illuminating. This can be found in all his work, but it takes its most prominent place in the aptly entitled The Philosophy of Freedom (London: Rudolf Steiner Press, 2001). 7. Usually a T (for transgender) is added, making it GLBT. I’ve left it out here not to exclude, but so as not to equate all the freedoms, pressures, and social issues that come with being a transgendered person—especially when it comes to awareness of the body. So much more can be said about the deep understanding of the body that transgendered public figures (writers, performers, etc.) have brought to the world. For the purposes of this essay, I leave them out. I also leave the T out here because of a later story in this essay that recounts an encounter with my GLB college group. At the time, it was not truly conceptually inclusive of transgendered people. 8. The picture is, of course, more complex when historical factors (such as the invention of the terms homosexual and heterosexual) and cultural factors are considered. I’m writing mostly of the Western experience from the early twentieth century to the present day. 9. In the otherwise reasonable and progressive book, Empire of Illusion (Nation Books, 2009). 10. Again, members of the GLB community do this by coming to terms with the separations they feel between what their bodies’ wills and what society tells them. So it’s not surprising that many of those who have done pioneering work in mental health have also studied concepts gender and sexuality. The works of Jessica Benjamin are a good example. 11. A quick but perhaps grossly oversimplified example of what is meant here: a sign of health is being able to experience sadness about an event—say the loss of a pet—but being able to make a joke about it later without abandoning the grief . And to eventually feel “better” without losing the ability to reflect with love and sadness on the lost pet. One state does not disqualify the other, and one state is not better than the other, because a person can move amongst them. 12. David Loftus, Watching Sex: How Men Really Respond to Pornography (New York: De Capo, 2002). 13. I don’t imply here that all spiritual systems get it wrong, only that it becomes even easier to miss mistakes about the object-body in non-Abrahamic traditions, because many are considered to be comparatively “peaceful” or “progressive.” This, of course, is the same sort of misconception that leads people to think that science is purely objective. 14. A good and quick exploration of this is featured in Martha Stout’s excellent book, The Sociopath Next Door (New York: Three Rivers Press, 2006). 15. Of course, the object-body isn’t all good, nor is it evil. There are individuals who have distorted impulses in their object-bodies and respond erotically and sexually—for not entirely understood reasons—to violence. I don’t mean to abandon exceptions here, but I don’t wish to dwell on them, either. CHAPTER 26 1. C.
